coredata.dc:description: The heat exchanger in multi-stage flash seawater desalination needs to be pickled during long-term service to remove oxidation products on the copper surface. In this paper, we introduce a method for preparing carbon quantum dots from biowaste, and explore their ability to inhibit the corrosion of copper in sulfuric acid and its corrosion inhibition mechanism. Firstly, the UV–vis spectra, microstructure, tissue composition of N, S-CDs were tested by Ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer, TEM and Fourier transform infrared. Secondly, the effectiveness of N, S-CDs in preventing corrosion was analyzed by electrochemical testing. The electrochemical test results showed that the impedance value of the solution of 200 mg/L N, S-CDs was 94,440 Ω cm2 at 298 K， with corrosion inhibition efficiencies (η) of 99.89 %, 99.77 % and 99.72 % at 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K, respectively. Moreover, SEM, XPS and AFM were used to characterize the micro-morphology and composition both before and after corrosion. It is worth noting that we used the fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes to deeply verify the corrosion inhibition mechanism of N, S-CDs on copper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The heat exchanger in multi-stage flash seawater desalination needs to be pickled during long-term service to remove oxidation products on the copper surface. In this paper, we introduce a method for preparing carbon quantum dots from biowaste, and explore their ability to inhibit the corrosion of copper in sulfuric acid and its corrosion inhibition mechanism. Firstly, the UV–vis spectra, microstructure, tissue composition of N, S-CDs were tested by Ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer, TEM and Fourier transform infrared. Secondly, the effectiveness of N, S-CDs in preventing corrosion was analyzed by electrochemical testing. The electrochemical test results showed that the impedance value of the solution of 200 mg/L N, S-CDs was 94,440 Ω cm2 at 298 K， with corrosion inhibition efficiencies (η) of 99.89 %, 99.77 % and 99.72 % at 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K, respectively. Moreover, SEM, XPS and AFM were used to characterize the micro-morphology and composition both before and after corrosion. It is worth noting that we used the fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes to deeply verify the corrosion inhibition mechanism of N, S-CDs on copper.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is always a notable problem that perplexes industrial development. Every year, there are numerous economic losses and safety hazards caused by metal corrosion [1–4]. Copper and its alloys for extensive application in electric wires, heat exchangers and pipes for their excellent corrosion resistance, conductivity, heat conductivity, processability and toughness [5–7].

Seawater desalination is one of the important means to solve the global water shortage problem. As the core equipment in the seawater desalination system, the performance of heat exchangers directly affects the efficiency and quality of seawater desalination. As a widely used heat exchanger material, copper is favored for its excellent thermal conductivity and corrosion resistance. However, during long-term contact with seawater, oxide layers and dirt are easily formed on the copper surface. These substances will seriously affect the heat exchange performance of copper, and thus affect the efficiency of the entire seawater desalination system. The heat exchanger in multi-stage flash seawater desalination is prone to great corrosion during long-term service [8]. When copper comes into contact with the atmosphere, an oxide film with corrosion resistance can be formed on its surface [9]. However, corrosion still exists on the metal surface in certain corrosive environment, which seriously affects its service performance. Pickling is a widely used technology in industry [10–13]. Because sulfuric acid has the advantages of high concentration and high density, sulfuric acid is often used as a pickling agent in the pickling process. Although pickling method can remove oxide layer and oily dirt on metal surface, but in the pickling process, it is bound to cause some corrosion of the metal. The addition of an inhibitor to an acid solution gradually become an important means of slowing down the corrosion of metals.

Corrosion inhibitors are classified as either inorganic or organic [14–16]. Inorganic inhibitors are widely used, but have many problems such as large dosage, bad corrosion resistance and severe environment pollution, so their usage has a significant constraint. Recently, the use of organic reagents to block copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 has been widely used. A vast number of studies have demonstrated that heterocycles such as N, O, and S, which are commonly found in organic inhibitors, can be easily bonded to a metal surface [17–23]. So far, many copper corrosion inhibitors have been identified, including imidazole compounds [9,24–26], expired medications [27–30], thiophene compounds [31,32], amino acids [33–35] and so on. Among them, Wazzan et al. [36]. used triazole derivatives as corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel and pure copper in the simulated desalination process, Singh et al. [37]. used imidazolyl ionic liquid as a corrosion inhibitor for copper‑nickel alloys in the descaling of a simulated desalination plant, and Mahdy et al. [38]. also used tetrazolium derivatives to inhibit copper and brass in simulated seawater. However, even if these inhibitors can achieve high corrosion inhibition efficiency, but there still serious pollution, chemical reagents overuse and other problems. Because of the high cost and low water solubility of traditional copper inhibitors, it is an urgent task to develop new copper inhibitors with low cost and green security.

The applications of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in biomedicine, electrochemical sensing, and metal corrosion prevention are extensive due to their excellent luminescence, good biocompatibility and chemical stability [39,40]. Based on this, by doping CQDs, the surface structure is controlled, the active center is expanded and the fluorescence quantum efficiency is improved [41,42]. In recent years, the doped CQDs are considered as a new kind of efficient corrosion inhibitors because of their low toxicity and water solubility [43–45]. Compared with traditional organic inhibitors, the doped carbon quantum dots have higher corrosion inhibition and environmental protection properties. So far, Cen et al. [46]. synthesis of N, S-CDs from thiourea and aminosalicylate by hydrothermal method. For the 5052Al, the carbon quantum dots have been used in hydrochloric acid as a corrosion inhibitor. It was found that N, S-CDs were effective in protecting Al alloys. When 5 mg/L the carbon quantum dots were added, the η reached 85 %. Saraswat et al. [47]. solvent heat treatment of pyromellitic acid at 180 °C using thiourea, urea and EDTA，the N-CDs (CD1) and N-CDs (CD2) had the η of 94.5 % and 89.2 %, respectively. Zhang et al. [45]. synthesized a mixed environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitor (SQDs) containing Sb, Sb2O3 and Sb2O4 by electrochemical stripping method. The inhibition efficiency of SQDS to Q235 steel in H2SO4 was study. The outcomes of the investigation demonstrated that SQDs' effectiveness in inhibiting to Q235 steel was 96.4 % at 200 mg/L amount. However, the use of these organic and inorganic reagents can result in not only waste and expensive costs for chemical reagents, but also environmental damage. In recent years, inspired by the notion of green chemistry, substantial advances have been made in producing carbon quantum dots from cheap, environmentally friendly raw materials. For example, Cui et al. [48]. synthesized N-CDs from green material dopamine. It was found that the inhibitor effect in HCL was 96.1 % at 400 ppm; Zheng et al. [49]. using grapefruit peel as raw material, BCQDs were obtained by hydrothermal synthesis, centrifugation, dialysis and freeze-drying, Q235 steel has an inhibitory effectiveness of up to 95.9 % in HCL. Long et al. [50]. with litchi leaves as the precursor, carbon quantum dots (LCD) synthesized by hydroheat have obvious photoluminescence and stable dispersion. In addition, 200 mg/L LCD can achieve 98.06 % inhibition efficiency for Q235 steel in 1 M HCL. However, the corrosion prevention capabilities of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 have seldom been examined using carbon quantum dots produced using biomass as the precursor.

In this article, the N, S-CDs were synthesized by a simple solvothermal reaction using bio-waste chestnut spiny shells as raw material, ethanol as solvent and addition of thiourea and their ability to suppress corrosion on copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 was examined using electrochemical testing, and the maximum effectiveness of inhibition was 99.89 %, offering a superior copper inhibitor for the solution. At the same time, the corrosion inhibition mechanism was analyzed by corrosion morphology and its fluorescent properties, and it was concluded that N, S-CDs achieved corrosion inhibition by forming an adsorption film. Most importantly, after reading a large number of literature, we did not see the preparation of carbon quantum dot corrosion inhibitor with chestnut thorn shell as raw material. This study is the first to demonstrate the remarkable excellent corrosion inhibition ability of carbon quantum dot corrosion inhibitors prepared from chestnut thorn shell, and provides an insight into its corrosion inhibition mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Chestnut shells are washed and dried after purchase. After drying, the chestnut prickly shells were thoroughly pulverized in a pulverizer and sieved through a 500-mesh sieve to remove the large particles, leaving a smaller powder for later use. Use an analytical balance to weigh 3.0 g chestnut thorn shell powder and 3.0 g thiourea, dissolved in 60 mL anhydrous ethanol, ultrasonic for 10 min to ensure that the thiourea thoroughly dissolved, and then transferring to the 100 mL PTFE hydrothermal reactor, will be sealed reactor transferred to the muffle furnace heated at 200 °C for 10 h. When the heating was finished and cooled down overnight, all of the resulting liquids were transferred to beakers and filtered out of large particles by a funnel. After the large particles were removed, the liquid was filtered through a 0.22 μm needle filter, and finally, the remaining liquid was transferred to a dialysis bag with a molecular retention capacity of 1000 Da for one day (the bag was boiled in boiling water for 10 min), during which the ultrapure water was changed every 6 h. After dialysis was completed, all the liquid in the dialysis bag was transferred to a refrigerator, and finally the frozen liquid was lyophilized in a freeze-drying oven into powder for spare use. Fig. 1 illustrates how N, S-CDs are prepared.

Using TEM (FEI-Themis G2 300), the dimensions and form of N, S-CDs were investigated. The UV–vis (TU-1810) absorbance of N, S-CDs was characterized using Ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer and the composition was analyzed by FTIR (Nicolet-iS50). Fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-2301PC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical testing and preparation of solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The three-electrode setup is used for electrochemical testing. The working electrode consists of a 10-cm copper wire and a 1 cm3 copper block (except for the processing area of 1 square centimeter reserved, the rest is coated with epoxy resin), the saturated calomel serving as the reference and the platinum sheet serving as the pair electrode. Before every test, the working electrodes were sanded with grades 400,800,1200 and 2000. All the electrodes were cleaned with anhydrous ethanol, and then dried with cold-air. The blank solution was diluted from 98 % H2SO4, and then add different amount of N, S-CDs as experimental solution.

The test duration for the open circuit potential (E
ocp
) is 2400 s. The next step involved testing the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using a 10−2 V sinusoidal potential signal that ranged from 105 Hz to 10−2 Hz. Finally, The Tafel polarization was scanned at 10−3 V/s with a potential range of E
ocp ± 250 mV. To guarantee good repeatability, every test was repeated three times in a thermostatic water bath with the same condition.

Copper samples (1 × 1 × 1 cm3 and 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5 cm3, Copper purity is greater than 99 %) are water-ground using 400, 800, 1200, 2000, 3000, 5000, and 7000 sandpapers until smooth and dazzling. Before use, the polished copper sample is cleaned with anhydrous ethanol, followed by cold air drying. The pre-treated samples were submerged in the blank solution or the experimental solution at 298 K, 308 K and 318 K. Copper samples (1 × 1 × 1 cm3) were used for atomic force microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy atomic force microscopy. Copper samples (0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5 cm3) were used for scanning electron microscopy.

The morphology and microstructure of nanoparticles are frequently observed using TEM. In this experiment, TEM analysis of the size and shapes of N, S-CDs, and the samples were distributed to the carbon film after dispersion by ultrasound. The TEM pictures, as presented in Fig. 2(a), (b), unequivocally demonstrate that synthesized N, S-CDs are evenly dispersed and have diameters less than 10 nm.

UV–vis plots of N, S-CDs are demonstrated in Fig. 2c, where two peaks at 248 nm and 320 nm are visible. These correspond to the π-π* leaps of CC and n-π* leaps of CO, respectively. The FTIR method is useful for the detection of functional groups of these compounds. In Fig. 2d, the absorption peaks of different functional groups in N, S-CDs are clearly observed. The typical expansion vibration peak of -NH and -OH is the wide seam at 3160 cm−1 [51]. 2920 cm−1 is the saturation CH [52]. At 2060 cm−1, the double bond union of OC and SC occurs [53]. The peaks of CC and CN are at 1610 cm−1 [54]. The peak at 1450 cm−1 is the CN [55]. The peak of the -COO is 1370 cm−1 and the peak of 1040 cm−1 might be due to C-SO3 [56]. The peaks caused by the CH bending vibration of aromatic or aliphatic compounds are located below 1000 cm−1.

Fig. 3 illustrates E
ocp curves for adding various content of N, S-CDs at various temperatures. By analyzing the E
ocp -time curve of the test solution and the blank solution, the curve of the experimental solution was found to be significantly more negative, which may be caused by the dissolution of the oxide layer on the copper surface [57], but tends to be stable at 2400 s, demonstrating that the current state of dynamic equilibrium has been reached by the adsorption and desorption on the copper surface. As the concentration rises, the E
ocp curve moves in a more negative direction. This is probably caused by the formation by N, S-CDs on the surface of the adsorption membrane of copper, which alters the solution's dissolved oxygen content [58].

To comprehend the reaction kinetics of anode and cathode of copper electrode, we carried out tafel curve test. Fig. 4 shows the tafel curve contain different amounts of N, S-CDs added at various temperatures. The following is known to be the copper's corrosion mechanism in 0.5 M H2SO4 [59].
(1)
Cathode reaction : 2

According to the reaction mechanism, the dissolved oxygen is reduced to OH− by electrons in H2SO4 system and then combined with H+ to form water. The anode is divided into two steps, the first step is that Cu+ ads are created when one electron is removed from the copper substrate, the second step is that Cu+ is converted into Cu2+.

N, S-CDs suppresses both cathodic and anodic branches, as seen in Fig. 4(c), (d), and (e), where the corrosion current densities of all cathodic and anodic branches were lowered by comparing the experimental solution with a blank solution [60]. At the same time, the corrosion current density is also significantly reduced, which suggests that adding N, S-CDs might considerably reduce the rate of corrosion. In addition, the cathode branch curves of all tafel curves are roughly unchanged, demonstrating that the cathode reaction mechanism remained unchanged when N, S-CDs were added. Noteworthy is the fact that after adding carbon quantum dots, the anode branches of tafel curves show a plateau, which is elongated as the amount of carbon quantum dots is increased. This might occur as a result of the N, S-CDs adhering to the copper electrode surface and forming a substantial layer of protection that causes the anode branches to passivate [56]. As the temperature rises, the passivation phenomenon gradually decreases. The findings demonstrated that adsorption of N, S-CDs on copper electrode achieved equilibrium, and certain desorption behavior appeared.

In order to further investigate the corrosion kinetics of anode and cathode of copper electrode, fitting the tafel curve by classical extrapolation to obtain corrosion current density (i
corr ), corrosion potential (E
corr ), anode and cathode slope (β

Table 1 makes it very evident that at the same temperature, i
corr and R
corr decrease with the grow in number of content. And when the concentration remains the same, i
corr , R
corr and E
corr increase with the rise of temperature. Therefore, higher temperature will accelerate copper corrosion [69]. Fortunately, at 318 K, N, S-CDs had a corrosion inhibition rate of 92 %. This demonstrates how tightly the N, S-CDs formed adsorption layer is adhered to the copper surface, even at 318 K temperature, it can still protect the copper base from corrosion. Table 1 shows that the E
corr of the experimental solution is significantly shifted over 85 mV in comparison with the blank solution [18,70,71], and N, S-CDs can be regarded as cathode inhibitor.

Fig. 5 illustrates the Nyquist curve for adding different amount of N, S-CDs at various temperatures. As showed in Fig. 5, as the concentration of carbon quantum dots increases, the capacitance reactance arc radius increases significantly, which shows that adsorbed N, S-CD molecules on the electrode surface enhance the resistance to charge transfer [72]. At the same concentration of carbon quantum dots, the arc radius of the capacitive reactance falls as the temperature increases, which is caused by the desorption of N, S-CDs molecules due to the increase of temperature. It should be observed that the blank solution curve has a truncated half-arc and a tilted tail. The slanted tail is cause by the warburg impedance, while the half-arc is related to the charge transfer resistance's double-layer capacitance. The reason for this is that Cu ions are diffused from the electrode to the solution, or that diffuse oxygen from the solution reaches the electrode. The addition of N, S-CDs caused the warburg impedance to vanish, suggesting that N, S-CDs were adsorbed on the copper, forming a dense protective layer, preventing the electrode from contacting the solution [73]. At the same time, the arc of capacitive reactance after adding N, S-CDs presents an imperfect semicircle, which is caused by the non-uniformity of copper surface [74].

Fig. 6 shows the Bode diagram of adding N, S-CDs of different contents at various temperatures. As can be seen in Fig. 6, as the amount of carbon quantum dots rises, all the impedance mode value increases and the phase angle increases. The larger the phase angle, the more carbon quantum dots adsorbed on the copper electrode [75]. In the Bode diagrams of 308 K and 318 K, it is evident from the phase diagrams that there are two peaks, which proves that N, S-CDs have two time constants for adsorption on copper surface. The first is caused by the relaxation of the double layer capacitance, and the second is the desorption of N, S-CDs [59]. As a result, a rise in temperature is quite likely to accelerate the thermal movement of N, S-CDs, resulting in separation on the materials surface.

To conduct a quantitative analysis and discourse on the EIS data, the analogous circuit depicted in Fig. 7 was used to fit film resistance (R
f ), charge transfer resistance (R

As illustrated in Table 2 , when the amount of N, S-CDs increases, so does R
ct , demonstrates the creation of an adsorption layer on the copper electrode. At the same time, C
f and C
dl are much lower than in the blank solution due to the substitution of N, S-CDs for water molecules on the electrode, which can be represented by formula (10) and (11) [87,88]:

Where the area of the electrode in contact with the solution is indicated by S; d is the thickness of the N, S-CDs molecular film on the electrode; ε is the local dielectric constant; ε
0 is the vacuum dielectric constant and F is Faraday's constant.

Formula (10) and (11) indicates that the drop in C
dl and C
f is produced by the creation of a protective coating on the electrode of the N, S-CDs molecules, which decreases the contact area between the solution and the electrode. Additionally, because organic molecules have a lower permittivity than water molecules, the adsorption of carbon quantum dots can lower the value of the local permittivity (ε). Moreover, because organic molecules have a volume that is obviously much larger than that of water molecules, so that the protective layer of carbon quantum dots becomes thicker. This cause in the inhibition of copper ion transport from the electrode surface to the corrosive medium., so as to limit corrosion. It was discovered that when the amount of carbon quantum dots increased, the η improved and at 298 K and 200 mg/L, the η was 99.89 %. It's worth mentioning that when temperature rises, the R
ct value falls, suggesting that the copper's corrosion will worsen as the temperature rises. Nevertheless, η remains about 99 % at 318 K, which shows that N, S-CDs is an excellent inhibitor, and it is possible to maintain good corrosion resistance in a given temperature range.

At different temperatures, following a 24 h immersion of the copper block in the experimental (containing 200 mg/L N, S-CDs) or blank solutions, and the SEM image is shown in Fig. 8 . There is quite severe surface corrosion on the copper soak in the blank solution, with obvious corrosion traces and corrosion pits, as demonstrated in Fig. 8(a), (b) and (c). The corrosion remnants get denser and more noticeable as the temperature rises. As illustrates in Fig. 8(d), (e) and (f), as the temperature increases, the copper surface corrosion increases. No obvious corrosion trace on the copper surface, and the scratches of the sandpaper are still clearly visible at 298 K. At 308 K, the surface is reasonably smooth, and the scratches are plainly visible, however there are some concave and convex corrosion marks. Scratches on the surface of copper at 318 K have almost disappeared, accompanied by corrosion cracks, but compared to the copper soak in the blank solution, it is still far smoother. It is worth noting that there are many densely packed small particles on the surface of copper after being soaked by the experimental solution at 298 K and 308 K temperatures, and at 308 K, there are far fewer tiny particles on the surface of copper than there were at 298 K. This is most likely caused by the fact that tiny molecules of N, S-CDs cluster on the surface of copper when they are adsorbed there. The thermal mobility of tiny molecules intensifies as temperature rises, which leads to the reduction of N, S-CDs particles on the surface of copper soaked in 308 K. Therefore, the outcomes show that t the N, S-CDs is effective in reducing copper corrosion in sulfuric acid, while the temperature increase is negatively correlated with its corrosion suppression capacity, which is consistent with the electrochemical experiments.

In the past several years, AFM has become increasingly popular for observing the microstructure of material surfaces because it demands a more relaxed working environment while maintaining excellent resolution. Fig. 9 is the 3D shape of copper's surface following 24 h of soak in the blank solution and the experimental solution (containing 200 mg/L N, S-CDs). Fig. 10 displays contour maps at various temperatures. The copper soak in the blank solution is uneven, as seen in Fig. 9(a), (b) and(c), with visible corrosion grooves appearing; the average roughness increases from 44.2 nm to 103.5 nm as the temperature rises, indicating a greater degree of corrosion on the material surface. Fig. 9(d), (e) and (f) shows the 3D shape of copper's surface after it was submerged in the experimental solution for 24 h at various temperatures. The fact that the copper's surface is comparatively flat and that there are still some faint signs of sandpaper sanding suggests that the metal has not experienced severe corrosion. Additionally, the copper's roughness level is only around 10 and 20, suggesting that carbon quantum dots can successfully prevent copper rusting in H2SO4. The temperature increased to 318 K from 298 K, the average roughness of the copper surface increased from 11.7 nm to 22.5 nm. This suggests that in solutions containing carbon quantum dots, the corrosion of copper was similarly exacerbated by temperature increases.

We introduced the XPS test to better understand N, S-CD adsorption on the copper. In order to investigate the surface adsorption process, XPS may be used to determine the chemical makeup of corrosion products. Fig. 11 shows the XPS maps of the samples soaked in the blank solution or in the experimental solution (containing 200 mg/L N, S-CDs) at 298 K for 24 h. In contrast to the surface composition of the copper pattern following immersion in H2SO4, as shown in Fig. 11a, the presence of N elements following immersion in the experimental solution indicates the presence of carbon quantum dots on the copper surface. Compared to variations in the amount of each element, the growth of C and O elements and the decrease of Cu content can also further prove that carbon quantum dots adsorb on the copper and make the Cu base less exposed to the outside to achieve corrosion inhibition. Fig. 11b shows the XPS peak of Cu 2p, with typical Cu 2p double peaks at 952.38 eV and 932.58 eV, corresponding to Cu (I) 2p1/2 and Cu (I) 2p3/2 [56], respectively, due to the presence of copper substrate and copper ion; Cu (II) still shows characteristic peaks at 934.98 eV and 955.03 eV [89], but they are significantly decreased, demonstrating that Cu(II) still exists even though N, S-CDs can impede the transition from Cu (I) to Cu(II). Fig. 11c shows the three peaks of C 1s, the C-C/C=C, C-O-C, and O=C-O are represented by the three peaks on the Cu-Bare map, which are located at 284.83 eV, 286.38 eV, and 288.33 eV [90], respectively, which may be the presence of accidental carbon pollution, and CN peaks at 284.83 eV in the Cu-CDs map, which is most likely the presence of N, S-CDs [91]. Fig. 11d shows the XPS map of O 1s. Compared with Cu-Bare, in addition to the CuO/Cu2O peaks at 531.88 eV, C-O-C and CO XPS peaks are found at 533.13 eV and 531.18 eV in the map of Cu-CDs [92]. For the S 2p spectrum shown in the Cu-CDs spectrum in Fig. 11e, the new peak at 163.63 eV is attributed to CuS [57], this suggests that the N, S-CDs can form chemical bonds with the copper surface in order to adsorb to it. Fig. 11f depicts an XPS map of the surface of copper bathed in the experimental solution. The three peaks at 401.38 eV, 400.43 eV, 399.33 eV correspond to CNH, NCu, and CN [49,50], respectively. N, S-CDs may be adsorbed on the Cu surface by building new bone, as shown by the existence of NCu and SCu bonds.

Due to the existence of specific fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots, to provide more insight into how N, S-CDs limit copper corrosion in H2SO4, we examined the fluorescence properties of the carbon quantum dot by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence intensity of 0.1 mg/mL N, S-CDs solution was measured at 365 nm excitation wavelength at different time intervals. As illustrated in Fig. 12 (a), (b), the fluorescence strength of the solution with or without the copper block was almost unchanged at 0 min, and the fluorescence intensity was 975 at 365 nm. It is important to note that, as Fig. 12a shows, the solution containing the copper block shows a notable decrease in fluorescence intensity throughout the course of the first 60 min. This is most likely because, as time passes, fluorescent carbon quantum dots adsorbed on the copper, lowering the concentration of the carbon quantum dot molecules in the solution and weakening the fluorescence of the solution; however, the change in the solution's fluorescence intensity is slowed down after 60 min, this is explained by the fact that the N, S-CDs molecules' adsorption on the copper achieved a saturated condition, slowing down the rate at which the molecules' adsorption occurs.

To further validate the reaction process, we added various concentrations of Cu2+ solution to the N, S-CDs solution, and Fig. 12c shows the trend of fluorescence intensity of the solution with the various amount of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 12c, as the content of copper ions grew, the fluorescence intensity dropped, demonstrating the phenomenon of copper ion fluorescence quenching of the carbon quantum dots solution. This results from the excited electron transition in N, S-CDs to the unoccupied empty orbit of the copper ion, making N, S-CDs combined with copper ions and leads to the disappearance of the fluorescence of the carbon quantum dots [93–95]. Since copper ions are difficult to exist as monovalent in solution, it is difficult to detect whether N, S-CDs are also coordinated with cuprous ions by this kind of fluorescence quenching phenomenon, but the XPS results proved the existence of monovalent copper and the content of divalent copper was significantly reduced, so we inferred that the N, S-CDs would also form coordination bonds with monovalent copper. Thus, in summary, by making coordination bonds with copper ions and cuprous ions, and copper substrates can be prevented from corroding by N, S-CDs molecules.

As shown in formula (12) and (13), the following corrosion inhibition mechanism was introduced based on a range of experimental results: the molecules and the copper substrate surface have a chemical adsorption effect, the N, S-CDs molecules containing N, S, and other heteroatoms with electron-absorbing capacity, and the copper existence of empty orbitals, can provide electrons in Fig. 13 , Therefore, N, S-CDs molecules can combine with copper ions and cuprous ions to create stable coordination bonds, build durable protective layer on copper, and further minimize the contact area between the copper substrate and the corrosive liquid to achieve corrosion inhibition.
(12)
n
N , S − CDs +
Cu +
→
Cu − n
N
S − CDs
ads +
(13)

N , S − CDs +

2 +

Through experimental and theoretical studies, the following conclusions can be drawn:
(1)
N, S-CDs were successfully synthesized, and the nanometer size was less than 10 nm, and XPS and infrared spectroscopy showed that the synthesized carbon quantum dots had abundant N, S functional groups.
(2)
The results of electrochemical experiments show that N, S-CDs have excellent corrosion inhibition ability for copper in sulfuric acid environment. It is worth noting that when the addition amount is 200 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition efficiency can reach 99.89 %. In addition, with the increase of corrosion temperature, N, S-CDs always maintain a high corrosion inhibition ability, which indicates that the synthesized N, S-CDs is an effective copper corrosion inhibitor.
(3)
The results of corrosion morphology analysis show that N, S-CDs can be effectively used as a protective barrier to hinder the corrosion behavior of copper after adsorbed on the copper surface.
